title: MacOS 13.2.1 + podman + podman compose + mongo - volumes are not synchronised
body:
### Issue Description

After upgrading into: 
- podman - 4.5.0
- podman-compose 1.0.6 
- qemu - 8.0.0

Mongo volumes are not synchronised

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. clone repo -> https://github.com/lukasonokoleg/podman-mac-mongo-volume-issue.git
2. execute ./install.sh
3. execute ./machine-init.sh
4. execute ./machine-start.sh 
5. execute podman-compose -f ./compose.yaml up -d


### Describe the results you received

- mongo container spins up -> it is possible to connect to it
- data/db & data/configdb folders are created
- mongo db data within data/db is not synchronised with host mac 

### Describe the results you expected

- Volume to be synchonise

### podman info output


comments.body:
As much as I could figure out -- it seems: 

- files from mongo container are not copied into machine -> checked it with ssh

Can you provide the `podman inspect` output for the created container?

AFAICT the the mongo image [defines two volumes at `/data/db` and `/data/configdb`](https://github.com/docker-library/mongo/blob/cf85c62df0c753f5c523826aa8b94b5550fb723f/Dockerfile-linux.template#L104) which means podman will automatically create a volume for these paths. Thus your `/data` mount will only contain the directories as the db is on a separate mount point.

To fix this you need to set `./data/db:/data/db` in your compose file as volume.

@Luap99 

Based on your comment - I've changed mountings in compose.yaml file. 

ROOTFUL CASE - still doesn't work - Within the pod I am getting error: 

```
chown: changing ownership of '/data/db': Operation not permitted
chown: changing ownership of '/data/configdb': Operation not permitted
```

ROOTLESS CASE - works with 2 things in place: 
On deploying container into machine to have variable
```
export PODMAN_USERNS=keep-id
```
&  on compose.yaml having
```
services:
  mongo:
    ...
    privileged: true
    ...
```

@Luap99  - For my needs I am ok with rootless - but shouldn't it work with rootful as well?

The macos volume only has your uid. As this is used on your host the chown inside the VM to a different user is not allowed.

I suspect you would need to run with something like `--user $UID` when run as root so the container does not try to switch users and sees the volume has already the correct user. But this stuff really depends on what the container image is doing.

Bottom line is you cannot chown paths that are mounted from the mac.
